questions.question: List and briefly describe the four major phases of C++ compilation: preprocessing, compilation, assembly, and linking.
keyPoints: Compilation: translates C++ to assembly language.
Assembly: assembles assembly into object code (.o).
Linking: combines object files and libraries to produce executable.

questions.question: Give an example g++ command that compiles a single source file main.cpp into an executable named app, enabling all warnings and debugging information.
keyPoints: -Wall enables all warnings, -g includes debug symbols.
If only an object file is needed, use the -c flag.

questions.question: Explain the process of building a multi-file C++ project using g++ from the command line. Include steps to (i) compile each source file to an object file, (ii) link the object files to create an executable, (iii) use appropriate flags for optimisation and debug, and (iv) demonstrate a simple Makefile that automates the build.
keyPoints: Compile each .cpp to .o: g++ -Wall -g -c file1.cpp -o file1.o (repeat for each source).
Link object files: g++ -o myprog file1.o file2.o -lmylib.
Add -O2 for optimisation, -Wall for warnings, -g for debug symbols.
A simple Makefile: 'all: myprog', 'myprog: file1.o file2.o', 'g++ -o $@ $^', 'file1.o: file1.cpp', 'g++ -c $<'.

questions.question: Discuss static versus dynamic linking in C++. Provide g++ commands to create a statically linked executable and a dynamically linked one, explain the implications on executable size, runtime loading, and symbol resolution, and mention common linker errors such as 'undefined reference' and 'multiple definition' and how to fix them.
keyPoints: Static linking: g++ -static -o prog_static file1.o -lmystaticlib; library code is copied into the executable.
Dynamic linking: g++ -o prog_dynamic file1.o -lmydynlib; the executable loads the shared .so at runtime.
Static yields larger binaries but no external dependencies; dynamic yields smaller binaries but requires correct LD_LIBRARY_PATH.
Undefined reference: missing -l flag or library path; multiple definition: global variables defined in headers; fix with extern declarations or #include guards.
